Notice Of Motion And Motion For Good Faith Settlement Determination (C.C.P. 877.6(A)(L))***Redacted***
Off Calendar May-09-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT DOE 2's Motion For Good Faith Settlement Determination (C.C.P. 877.6(A)(L)).

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Doe 2's motion for good faith settlement determination is granted.

Defendant Doe 2 is a non-profit organization that operates in the San Francisco Bay Area. Doe 2 is a defendant in four cases, including this one, where plaintiffs have brough negligence claims related to childhood sexual abuse allegations. All four actions were brought under AB 218, which revived all previously time-barred sexual abuse claims for a three-year period. Doe 2 filed an answer to each of the four complaints. Following mediation, Doe 2 and the four plaintiffs settled all of the claims against Doe 2 and executed settlement agreements on March 27, 2024. The agreements were conditioned upon receiving good faith orders in all four lawsuits. Doe 2 now moves for an order determining the settlement was made in good faith.

Under CCP 877.6, the court may determine the "good faith" of a settlement upon a noticed motion. The court analyzes the "good faith" of a settlement using the factors articulated in Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal. 3d 488, specifically (1) a rough approximation of the plaintiff's total recovery; (2) the settlor's proportionate liability; (3) the amount paid in settlement, (4) the allocation of settlement proceeds among plaintiffs; (4) a recognition that a settlor should pay less in settlement than he would if he were found liable after a trial; (5) the financial conditions and insurance policy limits of settling defendants; and (6) the existence of collusion, fraud, or tortious conduct aimed to injure the interests of non-settling defendants. (Tech-Bilt, supra, 38 Cal. 3d at 499.) Additionally, a settling defendant's financial conditions and insurance policy limits may control the analysis and override the other factors. (County of Los Angeles v. Guerrero (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1149, 1157-1158 (citing Schmid v. Superior Court (1988) 205 Cal.App.3d 1244, 1248).)

Here, plaintiffs allocated the settlement amount evenly among the four cases. (Declaration of Daniel Zamora, 10.) The settlement was the result of a full-day mediation, and there is no evidence of collusion or fraud to injure the nonsettling defendants. (Zamora Decl., 11.) Additionally, the settlement amount is less than a judgment against Doe 2 would have been and is proportionate to its liability. Even if none of these were true, the analysis of Doe 2's financial conditions and lack of insurance coverage is dispositive in this case. Doe 2 is a nonprofit corporation. (Declaration of Rick Ohren, 2.) Doe 2 has been unable to secure insurance coverage for any of the four plaintiffs' claims. (Id., 7.) The settlement amount is the most that Doe 2 can reasonably afford to pay to resolve the four cases, while staying in business, and is essentially half of its current net worth. (Id., 8.)

In an untimely opposition, co-defendant Doe 1 argues that (1) Doe 2 should have provided the court and Doe 1 information related to its efforts to ascertain insurance coverage and (2) Doe 2's potential liability for indemnity to Doe 1 should override the other Tech-Bilt factors. Doe 1 cites no authority that a moving party must show that it exhausted all efforts to acquire insurance coverage before it moves for a good faith settlement determination. Instead, any party "asserting the lack of good faith has the burden of proof on that issue." (CCP 877.6(d).) As for Doe 2's potential indemnity to Doe 1, Doe 2's financial conditions and lack of insurance coverage would override this factor as well.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
